title: The Leaked Memo
briefing: A blunt internal memo with your initials on it leaked overnight, and it says the quiet part loud about a policy you've been selling differently in public. The press wants to know if it's real before the evening broadcast. You can confirm it and reframe, or call it a forgery and dare them to prove otherwise.

choices.label: Confirm it and explain the thinking
choices.result: You own the memo, walk reporters through the reasoning, and reframe it as candor. The contradiction stings for a day, then the story moves on.

news.headline: President confirms memo, defends internal debate as healthy
At least this White House admits what it's doing
Memo confirms the gap between words and deeds
POTUS says YEAH I WROTE THAT, deal with it 😤

social.text: admitting it is annoyingly the smart move here. respect.
wish the public message matched the memo but ok, honesty counts

choices.advisor: If it's real, confirm it before someone else defines it for you.

choices.label: Call it a fabrication
choices.result: You flatly deny it. Two days later the original document surfaces with metadata, and now the lie is the story instead of the memo.

news.headline: BUSTED: White House denied a memo it actually wrote
Document metadata contradicts presidential denial
Denying real documents only feeds the wolves
LIAR LIAR: the MEMO was REAL the WHOLE TIME 🔥📄

social.text: the metadata doesn't lie. the president apparently does.

choices.advisor: Never deny a document you can't un-write. The forensics always win.

title: Your Appointee's Side Hustle
briefing: Your new energy secretary still holds a fat stake in a company that just won a federal contract. The optics are a gift to your opponents, and she insists it's all technically legal. You can force her to divest or step aside, or let her ride it out and absorb the headlines.

choices.label: Order full divestment or resignation
choices.result: You give her 48 hours to divest or walk. She sells, the ethics office signs off, and the story loses its hook by week's end.

news.headline: Energy chief divests after conflict questions
Forced divestment is the bare minimum, but they did it
President acts fast to clear an ethics cloud
SELL SELL SELL: secretary dumps stock to keep her CHAIR 💺

social.text: divest or leave. that's the rule. glad someone enforced it.

choices.advisor: A clean appointee is cheaper than a month of conflict headlines.

choices.label: Defend her and call it a witch hunt
choices.result: You circle the wagons and dismiss the story as partisan noise. Then a second contract surfaces, and now the whole cabinet's holdings are under the microscope.

news.headline: President shields appointee as second contract emerges
Conflict-of-interest questions widen to full cabinet
Loyalty is admirable; this is reckless
SHE WON A SECOND CONTRACT?! cabinet stock check NOW 📈🚨

social.text: calling accountability a witch hunt is the oldest play in the book

choices.advisor: Defending one conflict invites a hunt for every other one. Don't.

title: Caught Lying To Congress
briefing: Your deputy chief of staff told a Senate committee something that turned out to be flatly false, and the transcript is public. Lawmakers are circling the word 'perjury.' You can cut him loose and let the process run, or vouch for him and gamble your own credibility on his.

choices.label: Accept his resignation, cooperate fully
choices.result: You accept his resignation, pledge full cooperation, and refer the matter out. Painful loss of a loyalist, but the institution holds and so do you.

news.headline: Deputy resigns amid false-testimony probe; White House cooperates
Lying to Congress should cost a job. It did
President chooses the rule of law over a friend
DEPUTY GONE: you don't FIB to the SENATE and stay 👋

social.text: he lied under oath and he's out. system worked for once.

choices.advisor: Loyalty to a person ends where loyalty to the office begins.

choices.label: Publicly vouch for him
choices.result: You stake your name on his honesty. A second contradicting document drops, and committee leaders pivot from his credibility to yours.

news.headline: President stakes credibility on aide who misled Congress
New document deepens false-testimony case against deputy
Tying yourself to a perjury risk is a rookie error
POTUS PINKY-SWEARS the liar isn't lying 🤞💀

social.text: now it's the president's word on the line. terrible bet.

choices.advisor: Don't lend your credibility to someone who already spent theirs.

title: Pay-To-Sit Dinners
briefing: A reporter has receipts: top donors who maxed out got private policy dinners and a say on a draft regulation. It looks exactly like access for sale. You can publish the meeting logs and tighten the rules, or insulate the donors and tough out the coverage.

choices.label: Release the logs, ban donor policy meetings
choices.result: You post every meeting log and announce a firewall between fundraising and policy. Critics grumble it should've existed already, but the access story collapses.

news.headline: White House publishes donor logs, bars access dinners
Transparency on big money? We'll take the win
A firewall on access cools a damaging story
EVERY donor dinner DUMPED online 🍽️📜 receipts for DAYS

social.text: publishing the logs is how you kill an access story. nice.

choices.advisor: Disclosure turns a scandal into a rule. Cheaper than a denial.

choices.label: Refuse to release the logs
choices.result: You claim the logs are private and protect the donors. A staffer leaks a guest list anyway, and now the cover-up is the story.

news.headline: STONEWALL: White House hides who bought the dinners
Leaked guest list undercuts White House secrecy claims
Hiding the donor list only confirms the worst read
WHAT'S ON THE LIST?! POTUS guards the GUEST BOOK 👀🔒

social.text: if it were clean they'd show us. it's never clean.

choices.advisor: A guarded guest list always leaks. Then it's secrecy plus the dinners.

title: Your Brother's Bad Deal
briefing: Your brother used the family name to land a sketchy overseas infrastructure deal, and a foreign partner is now bragging that he 'has a line to the President.' It's not illegal for you, but the smell is everywhere. You can publicly distance yourself and refer it for review, or quietly protect family and hope it stays small.

choices.label: Distance yourself, refer for review
choices.result: You state plainly that he speaks for no one but himself and ask ethics counsel to review any overlap. It's a tense family dinner, but the line holds.

news.headline: President disavows brother's overseas venture, orders review
Drawing a hard line on a relative? Surprisingly right
Family is family, but the office comes first here
POTUS THROWS BRO UNDER THE BUS 🚌 'he speaks for himself' 😬

social.text: told his own brother to pound sand. that's the standard.

choices.advisor: Blood doesn't get a security clearance. Put daylight between you now.

choices.label: Quietly shield your brother
choices.result: You stay silent and lean on people to drop it. The foreign partner doubles down on the 'line to the President' claim, and the deal becomes an international embarrassment.

news.headline: President silent as brother trades on his name abroad
Foreign partner repeats claim of direct line to the President
Protecting family at the nation's expense is indefensible
'A LINE TO THE PREZ' 📞🌍 the BRO deal won't DIE

social.text: every day of silence is the partner's free advertising. yikes.

choices.advisor: Silence reads as endorsement. Abroad it reads as policy.

title: The Vanishing Emails
briefing: An archivist flags that a month of staff emails covering a sensitive decision were 'auto-deleted' under a policy nobody remembers approving. It might be sloppiness, it might be worse. You can order a forensic recovery and report it, or quietly let the gap stand and call it routine retention.

choices.label: Order recovery and disclose the gap
choices.result: You bring in forensics, recover most of the messages, and disclose the lapse with the fix. Embarrassing on process, but it kills any cover-up narrative.

news.headline: White House recovers deleted emails, discloses retention lapse
Recovering the record beats shredding it
Owning a process failure defuses a ticking bomb
FROM THE DEAD: deleted emails RESURRECTED 💀📧 spooky

social.text: they recovered them instead of memory-holing it. correct call.

choices.advisor: A recovered record is a story for a day. A missing one haunts you for months.

choices.label: Call it routine and let the gap stand
choices.result: You shrug it off as standard retention. A backup turns up on a contractor's server, proving the deletion wasn't routine at all.

news.headline: COVER-UP: backup proves emails weren't routinely deleted
Contractor backup contradicts 'routine retention' claim
A convenient deletion is the reddest of flags
THE BACKUP EXISTS 😱💾 'routine' my FOOT 🚩🚩

social.text: there is ALWAYS a backup. they should know this by now.

choices.advisor: Nothing is ever truly deleted. Pretending otherwise is the trap.

title: The Whisper Campaign
briefing: A tabloid is shopping an affair rumor about you, thin on proof but heavy on insinuation, and your team is split on whether to engage. Ignore it and it might fade, or it might fester. You can address it directly and briefly, or refuse to dignify it and let the silence speak.

choices.label: Address it once, plainly, then move on
choices.result: You give one calm, flat denial and refuse a circus. With nothing new to chase, the tabloid's story deflates within the news cycle.

news.headline: President issues brief denial of tabloid rumor
A short, firm answer starves a smear
Rumor lacked proof; president didn't take the bait
POTUS shuts it down COLD 🥶 'next question' energy

social.text: one clean denial and back to work. that's how you handle it.

choices.advisor: Answer once, briefly, then never again. Engagement is oxygen.

choices.label: Launch an aggressive counter-attack
choices.result: You stand up a war room, threaten lawsuits, and attack the outlet by name. The escalation makes a thin rumor look like a story worth a week of coverage.

news.headline: President's furious response elevates a flimsy rumor
Legal threats keep tabloid story in the headlines
Overreacting handed the smear a second life
WHY SO MAD?? 😏 the LADY doth protest TOO MUCH 🔥

social.text: screaming about it is how a nothing rumor trends for a week

choices.advisor: The angrier you get, the more real the rumor looks. Don't feed it.

title: A Pardon For Sale?
briefing: A lobbyist is reportedly telling clients that a generous 'legal defense fund' donation could grease a presidential pardon for a convicted financier. You never authorized any such thing, but the whisper is spreading and one name on the pardon list happens to match. You can refer it to the special counsel and freeze the list, or wave it off as gossip and proceed.

choices.label: Refer to special counsel, freeze the list
choices.result: You freeze the pardon list and hand the matter to an independent prosecutor before lunch. The decisiveness signals you have nothing to hide and quarantines the rot.

news.headline: President refers pardon-for-cash claim to special counsel
Freezing the list was the only honest move
Self-referral signals confidence, not guilt
POTUS SICS the SPECIAL COUNSEL on his OWN pardon list 😳⚖️

social.text: freezing the list and calling in a prosecutor. that's how you stay clean.

choices.advisor: A pardon-for-cash whisper is radioactive. Quarantine it publicly, now.

choices.label: Dismiss it and proceed with the pardon
choices.result: You call it gossip and sign the pardon anyway. The lobbyist's voicemails surface, the timing looks damning, and 'pardon for sale' becomes a defining headline.

news.headline: PARDON FOR SALE: president signs as lobbyist tapes emerge
Voicemails raise questions about pardon's timing
Signing through the whisper was indefensible
CASH FOR CLEMENCY 💸🔓 the TAPES are OUT and it's UGLY

social.text: signed it anyway while the voicemails existed?? unbelievable.

choices.advisor: Of every scandal, this is the one that ends presidencies. Do not proceed.

title: Boxes In The Garage
briefing: A staffer found cartons of classified documents in your private residence's garage, apparently mishandled since the transition. Nobody's accused you of anything yet, but the archives and the press will both want answers. You can self-report to the archives and invite a search, or sit on it and hope a quiet return goes unnoticed.

choices.label: Self-report and invite a full search
choices.result: You notify the archives immediately, hand everything back, and consent to a sweep of the property. It's an embarrassing headline for a day, but cooperation is the whole story.

news.headline: President self-reports classified documents, invites search
Self-reporting beats getting caught, full stop
Cooperation turns a mishap into a footnote
CLASSIFIED in the GARAGE?? 📦🔐 at least POTUS FESSED UP

social.text: called it in himself and opened the doors. that's the move.

choices.advisor: Mishandling is a mistake. Hiding it is a crime. Self-report today.

choices.label: Quietly return them and say nothing
choices.result: You arrange a discreet return and tell no one. A courier logs the handoff, the log leaks, and the silence makes routine carelessness look like concealment.

news.headline: Leaked log: classified docs returned in secret, never disclosed
Undisclosed document return raises concealment questions
A quiet return was the worst available option
SECRET HANDOFF EXPOSED 🤫📦 why HIDE it?! 🚨

social.text: every handoff gets logged. trying to hide it just adds a crime.

choices.advisor: A quiet return becomes a loud cover-up the instant anyone logs it.
